fields.brief_block: Even assuming arguendo that counsel should have requested the charge, Defendant failed to demonstrate prejudice. “[T]his Court has declined to presume prejudice in the context of an ineffective assistance of counsel claim based on attorney performance.” Bradley v. State, 318 Ga. 142, 144 (2024).
fields.opinion_block: Bradley asserts without elaboration that “prejudice should be presumed.” But Bradley ignores that “this Court has declined to presume prejudice in the context of an ineffective assistance of counsel claim based on attorney performance[.]” Keller v. State, 308 Ga. 492, 496 (2) (a) (842 SE2d 22) (2020); see also Reid v. State, 286 Ga. 484, 488 (3) (c) (690 SE2d 177) (2010).
fields.finding_analysis:
The Bradley opinion supports the proposition: in rejecting Bradley's argument that prejudice should be presumed on his ineffective-assistance claim, the court relies on and applies the quoted rule that “this Court has declined to presume prejudice in the context of an ineffective assistance of counsel claim based on attorney performance.” The quoted language appears in the opinion essentially verbatim.

One accuracy note (CLOSE quote check, not a substance problem): within Bradley the sentence is a quotation of Keller v. State, 308 Ga. 492, 496 (2020), and carries an internal bracketed period (“performance[.]”). The brief drops the internal attribution and presents the language as Bradley's own, but Bradley adopts and applies the rule to deny relief, so citing Bradley for the principle is accurate.
